title: File Permissions
description: Understand Linux file permission model including ownership, permission bits, octal and symbolic notation, special bits, and umask.
content:
## File Permissions

Every file and directory in Linux has three attributes that control access:

- **Owner** — the user who owns the file
- **Group** — the group assigned to the file
- **Permission bits** — what actions are allowed

There are three operations:

| Symbol | Permission | Octal Value | On Files | On Directories |
|--------|-----------|-------------|----------|----------------|
| `r` | Read | 4 | View contents | List contents |
| `w` | Write | 2 | Modify contents | Create/delete files inside |
| `x` | Execute | 1 | Run as program | Enter (cd into) |

Three audiences: **owner**, **group**, **others** (everyone else).

---

## Reading `ls -la` Output

```bash
$ ls -la /etc/passwd /etc/shadow /tmp
-rw-r--r--  1 root root   2048 Jan 15 10:00 /etc/passwd
-rw-r-----  1 root shadow 1024 Jan 15 10:00 /etc/shadow
drwxrwxrwt  2 root root   4096 Jan 15 10:00 /tmp
```

**Breaking down `-rw-r--r--`:**

```
- rw- r-- r--
│ │   │   │
│ │   │   └── others: read only
│ │   └────── group: read only
│ └────────── owner: read + write
└──────────── type: - = file, d = directory, l = symlink
```

**Examples explained:**

- `/etc/passwd` — `-rw-r--r--` — everyone can read, only root can write. This is safe because passwords are NOT stored here.
- `/etc/shadow` — `-rw-r-----` — only root and shadow group can read. Contains password hashes.
- `/tmp` — `drwxrwxrwt` — the `t` is the **sticky bit** (explained below).

---

## Octal (Numeric) Notation

Each permission has a value: **r=4, w=2, x=1**. Add them up per audience:

| Octal | Binary | Permission |
|-------|--------|------------|
| 7 | 111 | `rwx` |
| 6 | 110 | `rw-` |
| 5 | 101 | `r-x` |
| 4 | 100 | `r--` |
| 0 | 000 | `---` |

```bash
# Common permission sets
chmod 755 script.sh    # rwxr-xr-x — owner full, others read+execute
chmod 644 config.txt   # rw-r--r-- — owner read+write, others read only
chmod 600 id_rsa       # rw------- — owner only, private key
chmod 700 .ssh/        # rwx------ — owner only directory
```

---

## Symbolic Notation

Targets: **u**=owner, **g**=group, **o**=others, **a**=all.

Operators: `+` add, `-` remove, `=` set exactly.

```bash
chmod u+x script.sh       # Add execute for owner
chmod go-w config.txt     # Remove write from group and others
chmod a+r public.html     # Add read for everyone
chmod u=rwx,g=rx,o=rx dir # Equivalent to chmod 755
```

**When to use which?** Octal sets all bits at once (clean slate). Symbolic modifies specific bits without touching others.

---

## Special Permission Bits

### Setuid (SUID)

When set on an executable, the process runs as the **file owner**, not the user who launched it.

```bash
$ ls -la /usr/bin/passwd
-rwsr-xr-x 1 root root 68208 /usr/bin/passwd
```

The `s` in the owner execute position means **setuid is set**. This is how the `passwd` command can write to `/etc/shadow` (owned by root) even when run by a normal user.

```bash
chmod u+s program    # Set SUID
chmod 4755 program   # Octal: 4 prefix = SUID
```

### Sticky Bit

On a directory, only the **file owner** (or root) can delete files inside — even if the directory is world-writable.

```bash
$ ls -ld /tmp
drwxrwxrwt 2 root root 4096 /tmp
```

The `t` in the others execute position means **sticky bit is set**. This is why `/tmp` works — everyone can create files, but you can only delete your own.

```bash
chmod +t /shared     # Set sticky bit
chmod 1777 /tmp      # Octal: 1 prefix = sticky bit
```

---

## umask — Default Permission Mask

The **umask** determines default permissions for newly created files and directories.

```bash
$ umask
0022
```

**How it works:** umask bits are *removed* from the maximum permissions.

- Files max: `666` (no execute by default)
- Directories max: `777`

```
umask 022:
  New files:       666 - 022 = 644 (rw-r--r--)
  New directories:  777 - 022 = 755 (rwxr-xr-x)

umask 077:
  New files:       666 - 077 = 600 (rw-------)
  New directories:  777 - 077 = 700 (rwx------)
```

```bash
$ umask 022
$ touch testfile && ls -l testfile
-rw-r--r-- 1 user user 0 testfile

$ mkdir testdir && ls -ld testdir
drwxr-xr-x 2 user user 4096 testdir
```

Set umask in `~/.bashrc` or `~/.profile` to persist across sessions.

title: Process Management
description: Learn how Linux processes work, including PIDs, fork/exec, process states, signals, and job control.
content:
## Process Management

A **process** is a running instance of a program. Every process has a unique **PID** (Process ID). **PID 1** is `systemd` (or `init`) — the ancestor of every process on the system.

```bash
$ ps aux | head -3
USER       PID %CPU %MEM    VSZ   RSS TTY      STAT START   TIME COMMAND
root         1  0.0  0.1 169512 13092 ?        Ss   Jan01   5:23 /lib/systemd/systemd
root         2  0.0  0.0      0     0 ?        S    Jan01   0:00 [kthreadd]
```

---

## Fork and Exec

This is **how every process is created** in Linux:

1. Parent process calls **`fork()`** — creates an exact copy of itself (child process)
2. Child process calls **`exec()`** — replaces itself with the new program

```
Bash (PID 1000)
  │
  ├── fork() → Child Bash (PID 1001)  [exact copy]
  │              │
  │              └── exec("/bin/ls")   [replaces itself with ls]
  │
  └── waitpid(1001)  [waits for child to finish]
```

This is why every process has a **parent PID (PPID)**. You can see the tree:

```bash
$ pstree -p
systemd(1)─┬─sshd(800)───sshd(1200)───bash(1201)───vim(1500)
           ├─cron(500)
           └─nginx(600)─┬─nginx(601)
                        └─nginx(602)
```

---

## Process States

| State | Code | Meaning |
|-------|------|---------|
| **Running** | `R` | Actively using CPU or in run queue |
| **Sleeping** | `S` | Interruptible sleep — waiting for event (most processes) |
| **Uninterruptible Sleep** | `D` | IO blocked — **CANNOT be killed**, not even with SIGKILL |
| **Zombie** | `Z` | Finished executing but parent hasn't called `waitpid()` |
| **Stopped** | `T` | Paused by signal (Ctrl+Z or SIGSTOP) |

### Danger Signs

- **Many D-state processes** = storage or NFS is likely hung. The system may become unresponsive. These processes CANNOT be killed — you may need to fix the underlying IO issue or reboot.
- **Zombies** hold a PID slot but use **no CPU or memory**. A few are harmless. Many zombies = parent process is buggy (not reaping children). Can **exhaust the PID table** (`kernel.pid_max`, default 32768).

```bash
# Find zombie processes
$ ps aux | awk '$8 ~ /Z/ {print}'

# Find D-state processes
$ ps aux | awk '$8 ~ /D/ {print}'
```

---

## Signals

Signals are software interrupts sent to processes.

| Signal | Number | Meaning | Catchable? |
|--------|--------|---------|------------|
| **SIGTERM** | 15 | Ask process to stop cleanly | Yes |
| **SIGKILL** | 9 | Force kill immediately | **No** |
| **SIGHUP** | 1 | Terminal closed / reload config | Yes |
| **SIGINT** | 2 | Ctrl+C — interrupt | Yes |
| **SIGSTOP** | 19 | Pause process | **No** |
| **SIGCONT** | 18 | Resume paused process | Yes |

**Always try SIGTERM first. SIGKILL is a last resort** — it gives the process no chance to clean up (close files, release locks, remove temp files).

```bash
kill 1234          # Sends SIGTERM (default)
kill -15 1234      # Same — SIGTERM explicitly
kill -9 1234       # SIGKILL — force kill, last resort
kill -HUP 1234     # SIGHUP — often reloads config
killall nginx      # Kill by name
pkill -f "python app.py"  # Kill by pattern
```

---

## Job Control

Manage processes within your shell session:

```bash
# Run in background
$ long_task &
[1] 12345

# Suspend foreground process
$ long_task
^Z
[1]+  Stopped    long_task

# List jobs
$ jobs
[1]+  Stopped    long_task
[2]-  Running    other_task &

# Resume in foreground
$ fg %1

# Resume in background
$ bg %1

# Run immune to hangup (survives terminal close)
$ nohup long_task &
$ nohup long_task > output.log 2>&1 &
```

**Key point:** When you close a terminal, `SIGHUP` is sent to all its child processes. `nohup` makes the process ignore `SIGHUP`, so it keeps running. Alternatively, use `disown %1` after backgrounding.

title: Text Processing
description: Master the Unix text processing toolkit: pipelines, grep, awk, sed, sort/uniq, and I/O redirection.
content:
## Text Processing

### The Unix Pipeline

The pipe `|` connects the **stdout** of one command to the **stdin** of the next:

```bash
command1 | command2 | command3
```

**Why pipelines are powerful:**
- **No temp files** — data flows through memory
- **Memory-efficient** — each command processes a stream, not the whole file
- **Composable** — small tools combine into powerful workflows

```bash
# Count unique IP addresses in access log
awk '{print $1}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20
```

---

## grep — Search Text

**grep** finds lines matching a pattern.

```bash
grep "error" /var/log/syslog          # Lines containing 'error'
grep -i "error" /var/log/syslog        # Case-insensitive
grep -v "debug" /var/log/syslog        # Invert — lines NOT matching
grep -r "TODO" ./src/                  # Recursive search in directory
grep -n "function" script.js           # Show line numbers
grep -c "404" access.log               # Count matching lines
grep -E "error|warning|critical" log   # Extended regex (OR)
grep -l "password" /etc/*              # Filenames only (not content)
```

**Useful combinations:**

```bash
# Find errors, exclude noise
grep -i "error" syslog | grep -v "CRON"

# Search recursively, show context
grep -rn --include="*.py" "import os" ./

# Count occurrences per file
grep -rc "TODO" ./src/
```

---

## awk — Field Processing

**awk** splits each line into fields (by whitespace by default).

| Variable | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `$1` | First field |
| `$2` | Second field |
| `$0` | Whole line |
| `NR` | Line number |
| `-F:` | Set delimiter to `:` |

```bash
# Print first field (e.g., IP addresses from log)
awk '{print $1}' access.log

# Print username and shell from /etc/passwd
awk -F: '{print $1, $7}' /etc/passwd

# Filter: only lines where third field > 1000
awk '$3 > 1000 {print $1, $3}' data.txt

# Sum a column
awk '{sum += $5} END {print sum}' data.txt

# Print line numbers
awk '{print NR": "$0}' file.txt

# Multiple conditions
awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $7 != "/usr/sbin/nologin" {print $1}' /etc/passwd
```

---

## sed — Stream Editor

**sed** transforms text line by line.

```bash
# Substitute first occurrence per line
sed 's/old/new/' file.txt

# Substitute ALL occurrences per line
sed 's/old/new/g' file.txt

# Edit file in place (with backup)
sed -i.bak 's/old/new/g' file.txt

# Print only lines 5-10
sed -n '5,10p' file.txt

# Delete lines containing pattern
sed '/^#/d' config.txt          # Remove comment lines

# Insert line before match
sed '/pattern/i\New line above' file.txt

# Multiple operations
sed -e 's/foo/bar/g' -e 's/baz/qux/g' file.txt
```

---

## The Counting Pipeline

This pattern appears constantly in Linux administration:

```bash
sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
```

**Why sort first?** `uniq` only collapses **adjacent** duplicate lines. You MUST sort first.

```bash
# Count HTTP status codes
awk '{print $9}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn
  15234 200
   3421 304
    892 404
    234 500

# Count login attempts by user
grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log | awk '{print $9}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Top 10 largest files by extension
find / -type f | sed 's/.*\.//' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
```

---

## I/O Redirection

Every process has three standard streams:
- **stdin** (fd 0) — input
- **stdout** (fd 1) — normal output
- **stderr** (fd 2) — error output

```bash
# Redirect stdout
command > file.txt          # Overwrite
command >> file.txt         # Append

# Redirect stderr
command 2> errors.txt       # Errors to file

# Redirect both stdout and stderr
command > output.txt 2>&1   # Both to same file

# Discard output
command > /dev/null          # Discard stdout
command > /dev/null 2>&1     # Discard everything

# Pipe only stderr
command 2>&1 | grep "error"
```

**Common pattern — log everything:**

```bash
./deploy.sh > deploy.log 2>&1
```

title: User Management
description: Learn how Linux manages users and groups through /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group, and the sudo system.
content:
## User Management

Linux is a **multi-user** operating system. Every process runs as a user, every file is owned by a user, and access control is built on user and group identity.

---

## /etc/passwd — User Database

Every user account has an entry in `/etc/passwd`. The format is:

```
username:x:UID:GID:comment:home_dir:shell
```

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `username` | Login name |
| `x` | Password placeholder (actual hash in /etc/shadow) |
| `UID` | Numeric user ID |
| `GID` | Primary group ID |
| `comment` | Full name or description (GECOS field) |
| `home_dir` | Home directory path |
| `shell` | Login shell |

**Examples:**

```bash
$ grep -E '^(root|ubuntu|www-data)' /etc/passwd
root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash
ubuntu:x:1000:1000:Ubuntu:/home/ubuntu:/bin/bash
www-data:x:33:33:www-data:/var/www:/usr/sbin/nologin
```

### UID Ranges

| Range | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| **UID 0** | `root` — superuser, bypasses all permission checks |
| **UIDs 1-999** | System accounts (services like www-data, nobody, sshd) |
| **UIDs 1000+** | Human users |

**`/usr/sbin/nologin`** as shell = account **cannot log in** interactively. Used for service accounts that should never have a shell session.

---

## /etc/shadow — Password Hashes

`/etc/shadow` is readable **only by root**. It stores password hashes.

```bash
$ sudo cat /etc/shadow | head -3
root:$6$rounds=5000$salt$longhashhere...:19000:0:99999:7:::
ubuntu:$6$xyz$anotherhash...:19000:0:99999:7:::
www-data:*:18000:0:99999:7:::
```

**Hash format:** `$6$salt$hash`

| Prefix | Algorithm |
|--------|-----------|
| `$1$` | MD5 (obsolete, insecure) |
| `$5$` | SHA-256 |
| `$6$` | **SHA-512** (current standard) |

**Special values:**
- `*` = account is **locked** (never had a password)
- `!` = account is **locked** (password disabled)
- `!!` = password has **never been set**

---

## /etc/group — Group Database

Format: `groupname:x:GID:member1,member2`

```bash
$ grep -E '^(sudo|docker|www-data)' /etc/group
sudo:x:27:ubuntu,admin
docker:x:999:ubuntu
www-data:x:33:
```

Every user has:
- **Primary group** — set in `/etc/passwd` (GID field). New files get this group.
- **Supplementary groups** — listed in `/etc/group`. Unlimited number.

```bash
# See your groups
$ id
uid=1000(ubuntu) gid=1000(ubuntu) groups=1000(ubuntu),27(sudo),999(docker)

# See another user's groups
$ groups ubuntu
ubuntu : ubuntu sudo docker

# Add user to supplementary group
$ sudo usermod -aG docker ubuntu    # -a = append, -G = supplementary
```

**Warning:** Forgetting `-a` with `-G` **replaces** all supplementary groups!

---

## sudo vs su

| Feature | `su` | `sudo` |
|---------|------|--------|
| Switches to | Another user entirely | Runs one command as root |
| Password needed | **Target user's** password | **Your own** password |
| Logging | Minimal | Full audit trail in `/var/log/auth.log` |
| Granularity | All or nothing | Per-command, per-user rules |

```bash
# su — switch user (need root's password)
$ su -                    # Switch to root
$ su - ubuntu             # Switch to ubuntu

# sudo — run as root (need YOUR password)
$ sudo apt update         # Single command as root
$ sudo -u www-data whoami # Run as specific user
$ sudo -i                 # Interactive root shell
```

**Edit sudo rules safely:**

```bash
$ sudo visudo
# Format: user  host=(runas)  commands
ubuntu  ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL
%sudo   ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL          # % means group
deploy  ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/systemctl restart myapp
```

**Always use `visudo`** — it validates syntax before saving. A broken sudoers file can lock you out of root access.

title: Filesystem
description: Understand the Linux filesystem hierarchy, inodes, hard links, symlinks, and how disk space really works.
content:
## The Linux Filesystem

Linux has a **single tree** rooted at `/`. Everything — disks, devices, processes, kernel parameters — is accessible somewhere under `/`. There are no drive letters.

---

## Filesystem Hierarchy

| Path | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/` | Root of the entire filesystem tree |
| `/bin` | Essential user binaries (`ls`, `cp`, `cat`, `bash`) |
| `/sbin` | Essential system binaries (`fdisk`, `iptables`, `mount`) |
| `/etc` | System configuration files (editable text) |
| `/var` | Variable data — logs (`/var/log`), mail, spool, caches |
| `/tmp` | Temporary files (cleared on reboot, world-writable with sticky bit) |
| `/home` | User home directories (`/home/ubuntu`) |
| `/root` | Root user's home directory |
| `/proc` | Virtual filesystem — live kernel and process info (not on disk) |
| `/sys` | Virtual filesystem — hardware and kernel parameters |
| `/dev` | Device files (`/dev/sda`, `/dev/null`, `/dev/tty`) |
| `/lib` | Shared libraries needed by `/bin` and `/sbin` |
| `/usr` | Secondary hierarchy — `/usr/bin`, `/usr/lib`, `/usr/share` |
| `/opt` | Optional/third-party software |
| `/boot` | Kernel, initrd, bootloader files |
| `/run` | Runtime data since last boot (PID files, sockets) |

```bash
# See what's mounted where
$ df -h
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1        50G   12G   35G  26% /
tmpfs           2.0G     0  2.0G   0% /dev/shm
/dev/sdb1       200G   80G  110G  42% /data
```

---

## Inodes — The Real File Metadata

An **inode** is a data structure that stores everything about a file EXCEPT its name:

| Stored in Inode | NOT in Inode |
|-----------------|---------------|
| Permissions | **Filename** |
| Owner UID/GID | |
| File size | |
| Timestamps (atime, mtime, ctime) | |
| Hard link count | |
| Pointers to data blocks | |

The **filename** is stored in the **directory entry**, which maps a name to an inode number.

```bash
# See inode numbers
$ ls -i /etc/passwd
131074 /etc/passwd

# See inode details
$ stat /etc/passwd
  File: /etc/passwd
  Size: 2048       Blocks: 8          IO Block: 4096   regular file
  Inode: 131074    Links: 1
  Access: (0644/-rw-r--r--)  Uid: (0/root)   Gid: (0/root)
```

### Inode Exhaustion

Each filesystem has a **fixed number of inodes** (set at creation). You can run out of inodes while still having free disk space — this means you have **too many small files**.

```bash
# Check inode usage
$ df -i
Filesystem      Inodes  IUsed   IFree IUse% Mounted on
/dev/sda1      3276800 250000 3026800    8% /
```

**Symptom:** `No space left on device` but `df -h` shows free space. **Check `df -i`.**

---

## Hard Links vs Symlinks

### Hard Links

A hard link is **another directory entry pointing to the same inode**.

```bash
$ echo "hello" > original.txt
$ ln original.txt hardlink.txt
$ ls -li
131074 -rw-r--r-- 2 user user 6 original.txt
131074 -rw-r--r-- 2 user user 6 hardlink.txt
```

Both names point to inode `131074`. The link count is `2`.

- **Survives deletion** — deleting `original.txt` only removes one directory entry. The data persists as long as any hard link exists.
- **Cannot cross filesystems** — inodes are per-filesystem.
- **Cannot hard-link directories** (to prevent loops).

### Symlinks (Symbolic Links)

A symlink is a **separate file that contains a path** to the target.

```bash
$ ln -s /etc/nginx/nginx.conf link_to_nginx.conf
$ ls -la
lrwxrwxrwx 1 user user 24 link_to_nginx.conf -> /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
```

- **Breaks if target is deleted** — the symlink becomes a dangling link.
- **Can cross filesystems** — it's just a path string.
- **Can link to directories.**

| Feature | Hard Link | Symlink |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Same inode | Yes | No (own inode) |
| Survives target deletion | Yes | No (dangling) |
| Cross filesystem | No | Yes |
| Link to directory | No | Yes |
| File size | Same as original | Length of path string |

title: Systemd and Services
description: Master systemd service management including unit files, targets, and the systemctl command.
content:
## Systemd and Services

**systemd** is **PID 1** — the first process started by the kernel. It manages:

- **Services** (daemons like nginx, sshd, postgresql)
- **Mounts** (filesystems)
- **Timers** (cron replacement)
- **Sockets** (on-demand activation)
- **Shutdown/reboot**

---

## Unit File Locations

| Path | Purpose | Edit? |
|------|---------|-------|
| `/lib/systemd/system/` | Distro-provided defaults | **Don't edit** |
| `/run/systemd/system/` | Runtime overrides | Temporary |
| `/etc/systemd/system/` | Local customisations | **Edit here** |

**Precedence:** `/etc` > `/run` > `/lib`. Files in `/etc` override the same-named file in `/lib`.

```bash
# See where a unit file lives
$ systemctl cat nginx.service

# Override specific settings without editing the original
$ sudo systemctl edit nginx.service    # Creates override in /etc
```

---

## Key systemctl Commands

```bash
# Current state management
sudo systemctl start nginx       # Start now
sudo systemctl stop nginx        # Stop now
sudo systemctl restart nginx     # Stop + start
sudo systemctl reload nginx      # Reload config without restart

# Boot behaviour
sudo systemctl enable nginx      # Start on boot
sudo systemctl disable nginx     # Don't start on boot
sudo systemctl enable --now nginx # Enable AND start immediately

# Status and troubleshooting
systemctl status nginx           # Current state + recent logs
systemctl is-active nginx        # Just "active" or "inactive"
systemctl is-enabled nginx       # "enabled" or "disabled"
systemctl --failed               # List all failed units
```

**Key insight:** `start`/`stop` = **current state**. `enable`/`disable` = **boot behaviour**. They are independent. Almost always you want both — use **`enable --now`**.

```bash
# After editing unit files, reload systemd
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
```

---

## Unit File Structure

A typical service unit file:

```ini
[Unit]
Description=My Application Server
After=network.target postgresql.service
Wants=postgresql.service

[Service]
Type=simple
User=myapp
Group=myapp
WorkingDirectory=/opt/myapp
ExecStart=/opt/myapp/bin/server --port 8080
ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
EnvironmentFile=/etc/myapp/env

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

### Section Breakdown

**[Unit]** — Metadata and dependencies:
- `Description` — human-readable name
- `After` — start after these units (ordering)
- `Wants` — soft dependency (don't fail if missing)
- `Requires` — hard dependency (fail if missing)

**[Service]** — How to run:
- `Type` — `simple` (default), `forking`, `oneshot`, `notify`
- `User`/`Group` — run as this user (don't run services as root!)
- `ExecStart` — command to start the service
- `Restart` — `on-failure`, `always`, `no`
- `RestartSec` — wait N seconds before restart

**[Install]** — Boot integration:
- `WantedBy=multi-user.target` — start in normal multi-user mode

---

## Targets (Runlevels)

Targets group units together. They replace the old SysV runlevels:

| Target | Old Runlevel | Description |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| `multi-user.target` | 3 | Normal boot, text mode, networking |
| `graphical.target` | 5 | Multi-user + GUI |
| `rescue.target` | 1 | Single-user, minimal services |
| `emergency.target` | — | Bare minimum, root shell only |

```bash
# See current target
$ systemctl get-default
multi-user.target

# Change default target
$ sudo systemctl set-default graphical.target

# Switch target now (like changing runlevel)
$ sudo systemctl isolate rescue.target
```

---

## Practical Examples

```bash
# Create a new service
$ sudo vim /etc/systemd/system/myapp.service
$ sudo systemctl daemon-reload
$ sudo systemctl enable --now myapp
$ systemctl status myapp

# Debug a failing service
$ systemctl status myapp
$ journalctl -u myapp -n 50 --no-pager
$ journalctl -u myapp -f    # Follow live logs

# List all running services
$ systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running
```

title: Monitoring and Performance
description: Learn to identify CPU, memory, and IO bottlenecks using vmstat, load averages, free, and lsof.
content:
## Monitoring and Performance

When a system is slow, the first question is: **what is the bottleneck?**

- **CPU-bound** — processors are maxed out
- **Memory-bound** — RAM exhausted, swapping to disk
- **IO-bound** — waiting on disk or network

Each has different symptoms and different fixes.

---

## Load Average

```bash
$ uptime
 14:30:00 up 45 days,  3 users,  load average: 2.50, 1.80, 1.20
```

Three numbers: **1-minute**, **5-minute**, **15-minute** averages.

Load average = number of processes that are **running (R) or waiting to run (in run queue) or in uninterruptible IO (D)**.

**Interpreting load for N CPU cores:**

| Load | N Cores | Meaning |
|------|---------|----------|
| 1.00 | 1 | 100% utilised — no spare capacity |
| 1.00 | 4 | 25% utilised — plenty of headroom |
| 4.00 | 4 | 100% utilised — fully saturated |
| 8.00 | 4 | **Overloaded** — processes are queuing |

```bash
# Check number of cores
$ nproc
4

# Or
$ grep -c ^processor /proc/cpuinfo
4
```

**Trend matters:** Load 1-min > 15-min = getting worse. Load 1-min < 15-min = recovering.

---

## vmstat — Virtual Memory Statistics

```bash
$ vmstat 1 5
procs -----------memory---------- ---swap-- -----io---- -system-- ------cpu-----
 r  b   swpd   free   buff  cache   si   so    bi    bo   in   cs us sy id wa st
 2  0      0 512000  64000 1024000    0    0     5    20  200  400 15  3 80  2  0
 1  3      0 256000  64000 1024000    0    0   500  1000  300  500  5  2 20 73  0
```

**Key columns:**

| Column | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| `r` | Run queue — processes waiting for CPU |
| `b` | Blocked — processes in uninterruptible IO (D state) |
| `us` | User CPU % |
| `sy` | Kernel/system CPU % |
| `wa` | **IO wait %** — CPU idle because it's waiting on IO |
| `id` | Idle CPU % |
| `si` | Swap in (from disk to RAM) |
| `so` | Swap out (from RAM to disk) |

### Reading the Patterns

- **High `wa`** → **IO bottleneck**. Disk is slow or overloaded.
- **High `us` + `sy`, low `wa`** → **CPU bottleneck**. Need faster CPU or fewer processes.
- **Non-zero `si`/`so`** → **Memory exhausted**. System is swapping — performance will be terrible.

---

## Memory — Don't Panic About "Used" RAM

Linux aggressively uses free RAM as **disk cache**. This is a GOOD thing.

```bash
$ free -h
              total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           16G         4G         512M        256M        11G         11G
Swap:           2G          0B         2G
```

**The important column is `available`**, not `free`.

- `free` = completely unused RAM (will be small — that's fine)
- `buff/cache` = RAM used for disk cache (can be reclaimed instantly)
- **`available`** = RAM available for new processes (free + reclaimable cache)

**Only worry when:**
1. `available` is near zero, AND
2. `si`/`so` in vmstat is non-zero (actively swapping)

```bash
# Watch memory in real time
$ watch -n 1 free -h

# Top memory consumers
$ ps aux --sort=-%mem | head -10
```

---

## lsof — List Open Files

In Linux, **everything is a file** — including network connections, pipes, and devices.

```bash
# Find which process is using port 80
$ sudo lsof -i :80
COMMAND  PID     USER   FD   TYPE DEVICE SIZE/OFF NODE NAME
nginx    1234    root    6u  IPv4  12345      0t0  TCP *:http (LISTEN)

# Find which process has a file open
$ lsof /var/log/syslog

# Find deleted files still consuming disk space
$ sudo lsof | grep '(deleted)'
java    5678 app  12w  REG  8,1 2147483648 131074 /var/log/app.log (deleted)
```

**Deleted files still consuming space** is a common gotcha. A file isn't truly freed until all processes close their file handles. The fix:

```bash
# Option 1: Restart the process
$ sudo systemctl restart myapp

# Option 2: Truncate the file descriptor
$ sudo truncate -s 0 /proc/5678/fd/12
```

---

## Quick Diagnostic Checklist

```bash
# 1. What's the load?
$ uptime

# 2. CPU or IO bound?
$ vmstat 1 5

# 3. Memory pressure?
$ free -h

# 4. What's consuming resources?
$ top -bn1 | head -20

# 5. Disk space?
$ df -h && df -i

# 6. IO stats per device?
$ iostat -x 1 3
```

title: Logging
description: Navigate Linux logging with journalctl, syslog, dmesg, and logrotate for effective troubleshooting.
content:
## Logging

Logs are your primary tool for understanding what happened on a system. Linux has multiple logging systems that work together.

---

## Key Log Files

| File | Contents |
|------|---------|
| `/var/log/syslog` | General system log (most services log here) |
| `/var/log/auth.log` | Authentication events — logins, sudo, SSH |
| `/var/log/kern.log` | Kernel messages |
| `/var/log/dpkg.log` | Package installation/removal history |
| `/var/log/journal/` | systemd journal (binary, use journalctl) |

```bash
# Quick checks
$ tail -f /var/log/syslog          # Follow live
$ tail -100 /var/log/auth.log      # Last 100 lines
$ grep "error" /var/log/syslog     # Search for errors
$ zgrep "error" /var/log/syslog.2.gz  # Search compressed rotated logs
```

---

## journalctl — The Modern Way

`journalctl` queries the **systemd journal** — a structured, indexed log store.

```bash
# View logs for a specific service
journalctl -u nginx
journalctl -u nginx -u php-fpm     # Multiple services

# Follow live (like tail -f)
journalctl -u nginx -f

# Last N lines
journalctl -u nginx -n 50

# Time-based filtering
journalctl --since "1 hour ago"
journalctl --since "2024-01-15 10:00" --until "2024-01-15 12:00"
journalctl --since today

# Filter by priority
journalctl -p err                  # Errors and above
journalctl -p warning              # Warnings and above

# Current boot only
journalctl -b

# Previous boot (useful after crash)
journalctl -b -1

# Kernel messages only
journalctl -k

# Output as JSON (for parsing)
journalctl -u nginx -o json-pretty
```

---

## Priority Levels

From least to most severe:

| Level | Keyword | Meaning |
|-------|---------|----------|
| 7 | `debug` | Verbose debugging |
| 6 | `info` | Informational |
| 5 | `notice` | Normal but significant |
| 4 | `warning` | Warning condition |
| 3 | `err` | Error condition |
| 2 | `crit` | Critical — service may be impaired |
| 1 | `alert` | Immediate action needed |
| 0 | `emerg` | System is unusable |

`journalctl -p err` shows levels 0-3 (emerg through err).

---

## dmesg — Kernel Ring Buffer

`dmesg` shows **kernel messages** — hardware detection, driver loading, OOM kills, filesystem errors.

```bash
# View with human-readable timestamps
$ dmesg -T
[Fri Jan 15 10:30:00 2024] EXT4-fs error (device sda1): ...

# Filter by level
$ dmesg -T --level=err,warn

# Watch live kernel messages
$ dmesg -Tw
```

**What to look for in dmesg:**
- **OOM killer** — `Out of memory: Kill process` — system ran out of RAM
- **Filesystem errors** — `EXT4-fs error` — disk corruption
- **Hardware errors** — `ata1: hard resetting link` — disk failing
- **Network issues** — `link is not ready` — NIC problems

```bash
# Check for OOM kills
$ dmesg -T | grep -i "out of memory"
$ dmesg -T | grep -i "oom"

# Check for disk errors
$ dmesg -T | grep -iE "error|fail|corrupt"
```

---

## logrotate — Prevent Logs Filling Disk

`logrotate` automatically rotates, compresses, and removes old log files.

Config: `/etc/logrotate.conf` and `/etc/logrotate.d/`

```
# /etc/logrotate.d/myapp
/var/log/myapp/*.log {
    daily              # Rotate daily
    rotate 14          # Keep 14 rotated files
    compress           # gzip old logs
    delaycompress      # Don't compress most recent rotated file
    missingok          # Don't error if log file missing
    notifempty         # Don't rotate empty files
    maxsize 100M       # Also rotate if file exceeds 100MB
    postrotate
        systemctl reload myapp > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
    endscript
}
```

```bash
# Test logrotate config (dry run)
$ sudo logrotate -d /etc/logrotate.d/myapp

# Force rotation now
$ sudo logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/myapp

# Check logrotate status
$ cat /var/lib/logrotate/status
```

**Common issue:** Logs filling disk because logrotate isn't configured for a new application. Always set up logrotate when deploying a new service.

title: Networking
description: Diagnose network issues systematically using ip, ss, dig, ping, and understand TCP/IP fundamentals.
content:
## Networking

### The Diagnostic Skill

When something network-related breaks, **work down the TCP/IP stack** systematically:

1. **Interface up?** → `ip addr show`
2. **Gateway reachable?** → `ping <gateway>`
3. **DNS works?** → `dig example.com`
4. **Destination port open?** → `nc -zv host port`

Don't skip steps. Don't guess.

---

## Essential Commands

```bash
# Show IP addresses on all interfaces
$ ip addr show
2: eth0: <BROADCAST,MULTICAST,UP,LOWER_UP>
    inet 10.0.1.50/24 brd 10.0.1.255 scope global eth0

# Show link state (up/down)
$ ip link show

# Show routing table
$ ip route show
default via 10.0.1.1 dev eth0
10.0.1.0/24 dev eth0 proto kernel scope link src 10.0.1.50

# Show listening ports
$ ss -tuln
State  Recv-Q Send-Q  Local Address:Port  Peer Address:Port
LISTEN 0      128     0.0.0.0:22          0.0.0.0:*
LISTEN 0      128     0.0.0.0:80          0.0.0.0:*

# Show listening ports WITH process names
$ sudo ss -tulnp
LISTEN  0  128  0.0.0.0:80  0.0.0.0:*  users:(("nginx",pid=1234,fd=6))
```

---

## Key Configuration Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/etc/hosts` | Local hostname → IP mapping (checked before DNS) |
| `/etc/resolv.conf` | DNS server configuration |
| `/etc/hostname` | System's hostname |

```bash
$ cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1   localhost
10.0.1.100  db.internal  db

$ cat /etc/resolv.conf
nameserver 10.0.1.2
nameserver 8.8.8.8
search example.com
```

---

## Diagnostic Tools

```bash
# Test connectivity
$ ping -c 4 8.8.8.8              # Ping by IP
$ ping -c 4 google.com           # Ping by hostname (tests DNS too)

# Trace the route
$ traceroute google.com

# DNS lookup
$ dig example.com                 # Query default DNS
$ dig @8.8.8.8 example.com       # Query specific DNS server
$ dig example.com +short          # Just the answer

# HTTP headers
$ curl -I https://example.com
HTTP/2 200
content-type: text/html

# Test if a port is open
$ nc -zv 10.0.1.100 5432
Connection to 10.0.1.100 5432 port [tcp/postgresql] succeeded!

$ nc -zv 10.0.1.100 5432
nc: connect to 10.0.1.100 port 5432 (tcp) failed: Connection refused
```

---

## Troubleshooting Methodology

| Symptom | Diagnosis |
|---------|-----------|
| `ping hostname` fails + `ping IP` works | **DNS problem** — check `/etc/resolv.conf` |
| `ping IP` fails | **Routing or firewall** — check `ip route`, firewall rules |
| Port unreachable | **Service not running** or **firewall blocking** |
| Connection timeout | Firewall dropping packets (not rejecting) |
| Connection refused | Port not listening — service is down |

```bash
# Full diagnostic sequence
$ ip addr show                    # 1. Do I have an IP?
$ ip route show                   # 2. Do I have a default route?
$ ping -c 2 10.0.1.1             # 3. Can I reach the gateway?
$ ping -c 2 8.8.8.8              # 4. Can I reach the internet?
$ dig google.com                  # 5. Does DNS work?
$ curl -I https://google.com      # 6. Does HTTP work?
```

---

## Tool Replacements (Modern Equivalents)

The old tools still work but the new ones are better:

| Old (Deprecated) | New (Use This) |
|-------------------|----------------|
| `ifconfig` | `ip addr` |
| `route` | `ip route` |
| `netstat` | `ss` |
| `arp` | `ip neigh` |

---

## TCP vs UDP

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Connection | Connection-oriented (handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery, retransmission | No guarantee |
| Ordering | Ordered delivery | No ordering |
| Overhead | Higher (headers, state) | Lower (minimal headers) |
| Use cases | HTTP, SSH, databases | DNS, video streaming, gaming |

**TCP** establishes a connection with a 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK), guarantees delivery, and retransmits lost packets.

**UDP** fires packets and hopes for the best — but it's faster and used where speed matters more than reliability.

title: Bash Scripting
description: Write robust bash scripts with proper error handling, variables, conditionals, loops, and functions.
content:
## Bash Scripting

### The Safety Net — Always Start With This

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
```

| Flag | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `set -e` | **Exit on non-zero** — script stops if any command fails |
| `set -u` | **Error on unset variables** — catches typos like `$NAEM` |
| `set -o pipefail` | **Propagate failures through pipes** — `cmd1 | cmd2` fails if cmd1 fails |

Without these, bash silently ignores errors and continues — leading to catastrophic bugs.

```bash
# Without set -e: silently continues after failure
rm -rf /important/data
cp /backup/data /important/   # This runs even if rm failed

# With set -e: stops immediately on failure
set -e
rm -rf /important/data
cp /backup/data /important/   # Only runs if rm succeeded
```

---

## Variables

```bash
# Assignment (NO spaces around =)
NAME="world"
COUNT=0

# Default values
NAME=${1:-"default"}          # Use $1 if set, otherwise "default"
HOST=${DB_HOST:-"localhost"}  # Use env var if set, otherwise "localhost"

# Command substitution
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
HOSTNAME=$(hostname)
COUNT_FILES=$(find /tmp -type f | wc -l)

# Arithmetic
COUNT=$((COUNT + 1))
TOTAL=$((PRICE * QUANTITY))

# String operations
FILE="/path/to/document.tar.gz"
echo "${FILE##*/}"    # document.tar.gz  (basename)
echo "${FILE%.*}"     # /path/to/document.tar  (remove last extension)
```

**Always quote your variables:** `"$VAR"` not `$VAR`. Unquoted variables break on spaces and glob characters.

---

## Conditionals

```bash
# String comparison
if [ "$VAR" = "value" ]; then
    echo "match"
fi

# Numeric comparison
if [ "$N" -gt 10 ]; then
    echo "greater than 10"
fi

# File tests
if [ -f "/etc/passwd" ]; then
    echo "file exists"
fi

if [ -d "/tmp" ]; then
    echo "directory exists"
fi

# String tests
if [ -z "$VAR" ]; then
    echo "VAR is empty or unset"
fi

if [ -n "$VAR" ]; then
    echo "VAR is not empty"
fi
```

**Common test operators:**

| Operator | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `-f FILE` | File exists and is regular file |
| `-d DIR` | Directory exists |
| `-e PATH` | Path exists (any type) |
| `-r FILE` | File is readable |
| `-w FILE` | File is writable |
| `-x FILE` | File is executable |
| `-z STR` | String is empty |
| `-n STR` | String is not empty |
| `-eq -ne -lt -gt -le -ge` | Numeric comparisons |

```bash
# Combined conditions
if [ -f "$CONFIG" ] && [ -r "$CONFIG" ]; then
    source "$CONFIG"
else
    echo "Config not found or not readable"
    exit 1
fi
```

---

## Loops

```bash
# Iterate over a list
for item in one two three; do
    echo "Processing: $item"
done

# Iterate over files
for f in /etc/*.conf; do
    echo "Config file: $f"
done

# Read file line by line (safe — handles spaces and special chars)
while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "Line: $line"
done < input.txt

# C-style for loop
for ((i=1; i<=10; i++)); do
    echo "Iteration $i"
done

# Process command output
ps aux | while IFS= read -r line; do
    echo "$line"
done
```

---

## Functions

```bash
# Logging function
log() {
    echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] $*"
}

# Die function — log error and exit
die() {
    log "ERROR: $*"
    exit 1
}

# Usage
log "Starting deployment"
[ -f "$CONFIG" ] || die "Config file not found: $CONFIG"
log "Deployment complete"
```

---

## Arguments

| Variable | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `$0` | Script name |
| `$1` | First argument |
| `$2` | Second argument |
| `$@` | All arguments (as separate words) |
| `$#` | Number of arguments |

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

if [ $# -lt 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <environment>"
    exit 1
fi

ENV="$1"
log "Deploying to $ENV"
```

---

## Complete Example Script

```bash
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

LOG_DIR="/var/log/myapp"
MAX_AGE=${1:-7}

log() { echo "[$(date +%H:%M:%S)] $*"; }
die() { log "ERROR: $*"; exit 1; }

[ -d "$LOG_DIR" ] || die "Log directory not found: $LOG_DIR"

COUNT=$(find "$LOG_DIR" -name '*.log' -mtime +"$MAX_AGE" | wc -l)
log "Found $COUNT log files older than $MAX_AGE days"

find "$LOG_DIR" -name '*.log' -mtime +"$MAX_AGE" -delete
log "Cleanup complete"
```

title: Boot Process
description: Understand the Linux boot sequence from BIOS/UEFI through GRUB, kernel, initramfs, to systemd.
content:
## The Linux Boot Process

Understanding the boot process helps you troubleshoot systems that won't start and understand what happens before your login prompt appears.

---

## Boot Sequence Overview

```
1. BIOS/UEFI POST
       │
       ▼
2. GRUB Bootloader
       │ loads kernel + initrd into memory
       ▼
3. Kernel Initialisation
       │ detects hardware, loads drivers
       ▼
4. systemd (PID 1)
       │ starts services, mounts filesystems
       ▼
5. Login Prompt
```

### Step 1: BIOS/UEFI POST

**Power-On Self-Test** — firmware checks hardware (CPU, RAM, disks). Finds the boot device.

- **BIOS** (legacy): looks for MBR (Master Boot Record) on first 512 bytes of disk
- **UEFI** (modern): looks for EFI System Partition (ESP), usually mounted at `/boot/efi`

### Step 2: GRUB Bootloader

**GRUB** (GRand Unified Bootloader) presents a menu and loads the kernel.

```bash
# GRUB config (auto-generated — don't edit directly)
/boot/grub/grub.cfg

# Edit this file instead, then run update-grub
/etc/default/grub
```

```bash
# /etc/default/grub — common settings
GRUB_DEFAULT=0                    # Boot first entry
GRUB_TIMEOUT=5                    # Wait 5 seconds
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT="quiet splash"
GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=""             # Kernel parameters
```

```bash
# After editing, regenerate grub.cfg
$ sudo update-grub
```

GRUB loads two files into memory:
- **`/boot/vmlinuz-*`** — the compressed Linux kernel
- **`/boot/initrd.img-*`** — the initial RAM disk

### Step 3: Kernel Initialisation

The kernel:
1. Decompresses itself
2. Initialises hardware (CPU, memory, interrupts)
3. Mounts the **initramfs** as temporary root
4. Loads drivers needed to access the real root filesystem
5. Mounts the real root filesystem
6. Starts **PID 1** (`/sbin/init` → systemd)

### Step 4: systemd (PID 1)

systemd takes over and:
- Mounts filesystems from `/etc/fstab`
- Starts services (networking, SSH, databases)
- Reaches the **default target** (usually `multi-user.target`)

### Step 5: Login Prompt

Once the target is reached, `getty` or a display manager presents the login prompt.

---

## Key Boot Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/boot/vmlinuz-*` | Compressed Linux kernel image |
| `/boot/initrd.img-*` | Initial RAM disk (initramfs) |
| `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` | GRUB configuration (auto-generated) |
| `/etc/default/grub` | GRUB settings (edit this one) |

```bash
$ ls -la /boot/
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  8.0M vmlinuz-5.15.0-91-generic
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root   90M initrd.img-5.15.0-91-generic
drwxr-xr-x 5 root root  4.0K grub/
```

---

## initramfs — The Temporary Root

**initramfs** (initial RAM filesystem) is a small temporary root filesystem loaded into memory. It exists because the kernel might need special drivers to access the real root filesystem.

**Contains drivers for:**
- **Encrypted disks** (LUKS/dm-crypt)
- **RAID arrays** (mdadm)
- **Network filesystems** (NFS root)
- **LVM** (Logical Volume Manager)
- **Specific disk controllers**

```bash
# List contents of initramfs
$ lsinitramfs /boot/initrd.img-$(uname -r) | head -20

# Rebuild initramfs (after adding drivers)
$ sudo update-initramfs -u
```

Without initramfs, a kernel compiled without the SATA/NVMe driver built-in couldn't read the disk containing the root filesystem — a chicken-and-egg problem.

---

## Kernel Modules

**Kernel modules** are pieces of code that can be loaded into the kernel at runtime — without rebooting. They're typically device drivers, filesystem drivers, or network protocols.

```bash
# List loaded modules
$ lsmod
Module                  Size  Used by
ext4                  745472  1
mbcache                16384  1 ext4
jbd2                  110592  1 ext4
nvidia               34816000  0

# Load a module
$ sudo modprobe vfat

# Unload a module
$ sudo modprobe -r vfat

# Get module info
$ modinfo ext4
```

**Persist modules across reboots:**

```bash
# /etc/modules — loaded at boot
$ cat /etc/modules
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
vfat
loop
```

---

## Boot Troubleshooting

```bash
# See boot messages
$ dmesg -T | head -50

# See systemd boot log
$ journalctl -b

# See previous boot (after crash)
$ journalctl -b -1

# Measure boot time
$ systemd-analyze
Startup finished in 3.5s (kernel) + 12.8s (userspace) = 16.3s

# See what took longest
$ systemd-analyze blame | head -10
```

title: Interview Q&A Bank
description: Practice common Linux interview questions with detailed answers covering filesystems, processes, networking, and troubleshooting.
content:
## Linux Interview Q&A Bank

These are commonly asked Linux interview questions with detailed answers. Understanding the *why* behind each answer is more important than memorising.

---

### Q: What is the difference between hard links and symlinks? What happens when the source is removed?

**A:** A **hard link** is another directory entry pointing to the **same inode**. A **symlink** is a separate file containing a **path** to the target.

When the source file is removed:
- **Hard link:** Data survives. The hard link still works because it points to the same inode. The data is only deleted when the last hard link is removed (link count reaches 0).
- **Symlink:** Becomes a **dangling link**. It points to a path that no longer exists. Accessing it gives "No such file or directory".

```bash
$ echo "data" > original
$ ln original hardlink
$ ln -s original symlink
$ rm original
$ cat hardlink    # Works — data still exists
$ cat symlink     # Error — dangling symlink
```

---

### Q: What happens when you run a command in the shell?

**A:** The shell follows this sequence:

1. **Parse** — shell reads the command line, expands variables, globs, aliases
2. **Fork** — shell calls `fork()` to create a child process (exact copy of shell)
3. **Exec** — child calls `exec()` to replace itself with the command's program
4. **Waitpid** — parent shell calls `waitpid()` to wait for the child to finish
5. **Exit** — child terminates with an exit code
6. **$?** — shell stores the exit code in `$?` (0 = success, non-zero = failure)

```bash
$ ls /tmp
# Shell forks, child execs /bin/ls, shell waits, ls exits
$ echo $?
0
```

---

### Q: What is the difference between a process and a thread?

**A:** A **process** has its own memory space, file descriptors, and PID. A **thread** shares memory space with other threads in the same process.

- Processes are **isolated** — one crashing doesn't affect others
- Threads are **lightweight** — share heap memory, faster to create
- Threads can communicate via shared memory; processes need IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory segments)
- A multi-threaded process shows as one PID but multiple entries in `/proc/[pid]/task/`

---

### Q: How would you troubleshoot a 404 error on a web application?

**A:** Work systematically:

1. **Check if the service is running:** `systemctl status nginx` or `ss -tulnp | grep :80`
2. **Check the access/error logs:** `tail -f /var/log/nginx/error.log`
3. **Check the URL path** — is the file/route actually there?
4. **Check file permissions** — can the web server user read the files?
5. **Check the web server config** — is the `root` directive pointing to the right directory?
6. **Check SELinux/AppArmor** — security modules may be blocking access
7. **Test with curl:** `curl -v http://localhost/path`

---

### Q: Explain RAID 0, 1, 5, and 10.

**A:**

| RAID | Method | Min Disks | Capacity | Fault Tolerance |
|------|--------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **0** | Striping | 2 | 100% | None — one disk fails, all data lost |
| **1** | Mirroring | 2 | 50% | 1 disk can fail |
| **5** | Striping + distributed parity | 3 | (N-1)/N | 1 disk can fail |
| **10** | Mirrored stripes (1+0) | 4 | 50% | 1 disk per mirror can fail |

**RAID is not a backup.** It protects against disk failure, not against accidental deletion, corruption, or ransomware.

---

### Q: Every command fails with 'command not found'. What's wrong?

**A:** The **`$PATH`** environment variable is broken or empty.

```bash
$ echo $PATH
# If empty or wrong, commands won't be found

# Fix temporarily
$ export PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

# Or use full paths
$ /bin/ls
$ /usr/bin/env bash
```

Common causes: corrupted `.bashrc`, bad `export PATH=` statement (overwriting instead of appending).

---

### Q: You ran `chmod 444 /bin/chmod`. How do you fix it?

**A:** `chmod` itself is no longer executable! Three workarounds:

```bash
# Method 1: Install coreutils to replace it
$ sudo apt install --reinstall coreutils

# Method 2: Use Python
$ python3 -c "import os; os.chmod('/bin/chmod', 0o755)"

# Method 3: Use Perl
$ perl -e 'chmod 0755, "/bin/chmod"'
```

---

### Q: What does a load average of 1.00 mean on a single-core system?

**A:** The CPU is **100% utilised** — exactly one process is running at all times, with no spare capacity. If load goes above 1.00, processes start queuing.

On a 4-core system, load 1.00 means only 25% utilisation. **Load average must be interpreted relative to the number of CPU cores** (`nproc`).

---

### Q: What is the difference between real UID and effective UID?

**A:**
- **Real UID** — who you actually are (the user who logged in)
- **Effective UID** — who the kernel checks for permissions

They differ when running a **setuid** program. Example: when you run `passwd` (owned by root with setuid bit), your real UID is your user, but effective UID is 0 (root) — allowing it to write to `/etc/shadow`.

```bash
$ id
uid=1000(ubuntu) euid=1000(ubuntu)  # Normal — same
# Inside a setuid program:
# uid=1000(ubuntu) euid=0(root)     # Different!
```

---

### Q: How would you check for unauthorized SSH logins?

**A:**

```bash
# Check auth log for successful logins
$ grep "Accepted" /var/log/auth.log

# Check for failed login attempts (brute force)
$ grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log | tail -20

# Count failed attempts by IP
$ grep "Failed password" /var/log/auth.log | awk '{print $(NF-3)}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Currently logged in users
$ who
$ w

# Login history
$ last -20

# Failed login history
$ lastb -20
```

---

### Q: How does HTTPS work?

**A:** HTTPS = HTTP over TLS. The process:

1. **TCP handshake** — client connects to server port 443
2. **TLS handshake** — client sends supported cipher suites, server sends its certificate
3. **Certificate verification** — client checks certificate against trusted CAs
4. **Key exchange** — client and server agree on a symmetric session key (using asymmetric crypto)
5. **Encrypted communication** — all HTTP traffic encrypted with the session key

Asymmetric crypto (RSA/ECDSA) is used only for the handshake. Symmetric crypto (AES) is used for data transfer because it's much faster.

---

### Q: What is the purpose of /etc/hosts?

**A:** `/etc/hosts` provides **local hostname-to-IP mapping** that is checked **before DNS**. Uses:

- Override DNS for specific hosts
- Define names for internal servers without DNS
- Block domains by pointing them to `127.0.0.1`
- Essential for `localhost` resolution (`127.0.0.1 localhost`)

```bash
$ cat /etc/hosts
127.0.0.1   localhost
10.0.1.100  db.internal db
10.0.1.101  cache.internal cache
```

---

### Q: How do you find all files larger than 100MB?

**A:**

```bash
$ find / -type f -size +100M -exec ls -lh {} \;

# More readable output
$ find / -type f -size +100M -printf '%s %p\n' 2>/dev/null | sort -rn | head -20

# Using du for directories
$ du -ah / 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -20
```

---

### Q: What is swap and what does heavy swap usage indicate?

**A:** **Swap** is disk space used as overflow when physical RAM is exhausted. The kernel moves inactive memory pages to swap ("swapping out") to free RAM.

**Heavy swap usage indicates:**
- Physical RAM is **insufficient** for the workload
- Performance will be **severely degraded** (disk is 1000x slower than RAM)
- Check with `free -h` and `vmstat 1` (look at `si`/`so` columns)

```bash
$ free -h
              total   used   free   shared  buff/cache  available
Mem:           4G     3.8G   50M    100M    200M        100M
Swap:          2G     1.5G   500M
# This system is in trouble — almost no available RAM, heavy swap

$ swapon --show
NAME      TYPE  SIZE  USED  PRIO
/swapfile file    2G  1.5G    -2
```

**Fix:** Add more RAM, reduce memory usage, or identify the memory-hungry process (`ps aux --sort=-%mem | head`).

---

### Q: What happens to orphan processes?

**A:** When a parent process dies before its children, the children become **orphans**. The kernel **re-parents** orphan processes to **PID 1** (systemd/init).

PID 1 automatically calls `waitpid()` on orphaned children when they exit, preventing them from becoming zombies. This is one of the critical responsibilities of the init system.

```bash
# Orphan processes get PPID = 1
$ ps -eo pid,ppid,cmd | grep myprocess
5678    1    /usr/bin/myprocess    # PPID is 1 — it's been re-parented
```

This is different from **zombies**, where the parent is alive but hasn't called `waitpid()`. Orphans are handled gracefully; zombies indicate a buggy parent.
